I'm considering a dessert table so guests can serve themselves. Any tips on quantity per person and presentation for a mixed-age crowd?

Good idea — a dessert table is festive and lets guests graze at their own pace. Below are practical guidelines for quantities, menu mix, presentation, accessibility, food-safety, serving tools, and a couple of sample setups (depending on whether you’re also serving birthday cake).

Quantity rules of thumb
- If you have a full birthday cake that will be served to everyone: plan about 0.5–1 extra dessert “piece” per guest on the table (for 60 guests → 30–60 pieces).  
- If the cake is small or the cake is decorative only: plan 1.5–2 pieces per guest (for 60 → 90–120 pieces).  
- For a mixed-age crowd, favor many bite-size items so guests can sample several without overeating; that also stretches portions.

Portion math examples (60 guests)
- Cake present + dessert table as supplement (aim 60 pieces):
  - Cookies (large): 24 pieces  
  - Brownie squares (2" x 2"): 18 pieces  
  - Mini cupcakes or petit fours: 18 pieces  
  - Include 8–12 labeled GF or dairy‑free pieces within these totals
- Cake not main (aim 90 pieces):
  - Cookies: 30  
  - Brownie / bars: 24  
  - Mini cupcakes: 24  
  - Fruit cups or petite tartlets: 12  
  - GF options: 12–15 included among the above

Types of desserts to include (mix for kids & older guests)
- Kid-friendly: mini cupcakes, soft cookies, small rice-krispie squares, bite-size macaroni-cupcake-style desserts, or mini brownies.  
- Older guests: softer-textured options and easy-to-chew items — pudding cups, mousse, flourless cake slices, soft cake or cheesecake bites, fruit cups, small custards.  
- Health/allergy considerations: fruit skewers or cups, nut-free cookies, and a small clearly labeled GF/dairy-free option (e.g., flourless chocolate brownies or GF lemon bars).  
- Visual variety: include a mix of chocolate, fruity, and plain-sweet options.

Presentation & layout tips
- Use tiers and varying height: cake as centerpiece on a raised stand, smaller trays/cake stands descending around it. Tiers save table space and look crisp.  
- Arrange flow so guests approach the table at one end and exit at the other; place napkins and plates at the end so guests can carry selections without blocking others.  
- Put the cake (if staged for cutting) in a place that won’t be confused with free-for-self-serving until after the official cake-cutting moment (or clearly label cake reserved for cutting).  
- Provide small plates (salad/dessert size) or do small paper plates to encourage single-portion grabbing. Consider half-plates for sampling.  
- Use clear labels for each item (name + allergens: contains nuts, gluten-free, dairy-free, vegetarian). Use small tent cards or printable stickers.

Serving tools & supplies
- One pair of tongs per item (or small serving spoons for bars) to avoid cross-contamination.  
- Cake server/knife reserved for the cake-cutting moment (handled by designated person).  
- Plenty of napkins and small dessert forks/spoons; consider disposable or reusable depending on your green plan.  
- Trash/recycling/compost bins nearby with clear signs.

Food safety & timing
- Keep cream-based or dairy desserts refrigerated until just before serving; don’t leave them out for longer than ~2 hours at room temperature.  
- Fruit cups/puddings are safe if chilled; use a chilled tray or place items on ice if the room is warm.  
- Have an observation volunteer periodically tidy the table and remove items that have been out too long.

Accessibility & safety
- Place the dessert table at a comfortable height for older relatives and leave some chairs nearby.  
- Avoid small hard candies or whole nuts for younger kids (choking hazard); if you include nuts, label and place them away from the kids’ station.  
- Make sure one clear GF area or tray of GF items is separated and has its own tongs to limit cross-contact.

Replenishment & leftover plan
- Prep a second set of trays in the kitchen so volunteers can quickly replace empty platters.  
- Assign one volunteer to monitor the table and replenish, tidy, and remove items after 90–120 minutes.  
- Plan for leftovers: have containers/foil ready and decide whether leftovers will be sent home with guests, given to a food shelter (check their acceptance policy), or packed for volunteers.

Sample compact menu for a cake + dessert table (aim 60 extra pieces)
- Birthday sheet cake: 60 portions (for formal cake-cutting).  
- Dessert table (60 pieces total):
  - 24 assorted cookies (chocolate chip & sugar)  
  - 18 brownie squares (cut small)  
  - 18 mini cupcakes (vanilla & chocolate)  
  - Make 10 of the 60 pieces GF (e.g., flourless chocolate brownies or labeled GF cookies)  
  - Add a fruit platter or 12 small fruit cups for a fresher option
